CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The teachers have a tough job in hand to beat
Starkie and Whelp, and the rank and file had better
keep their heads cool.  Resolutions nor letters to the
newspapers won’t do it, and they are not keeping their
heads cool if they place unreserved confidence in the
sensation-mongering of Murphy’s Ha-penng Dreadful.
What does William Martin Murphy care for the
National Teachers? About as much as he cares for
his unfortunate Tramway men. If he can make a
passing sensation out of the Mansfield dismissal he
will do it, and when the sensation has run itself out he
will let the teachers fight their own battle. It is

quite ecasy to test the sincerity of the ‘‘ Ha’penny

Dreadful * in reference to the Mansfield case. There

was another dismissal quite recently rather on all fours

with the Mansfield case. Mr. Mansfield is an excep-

tional teacher; Mr. W. P. Partridge, a skilled work-

man in the Inchicore Works of the Great Sourface Rail-

way, is an exceptional workman; both men are fighting

for their comrades and their class. Probably Starkie

and Whelp were only too anxious to get a chance to

dismiss Mansfield, and we have no doubt that the

authorities of the Great Sourface Railway, of which

William Martin Murphy, owner of the ‘‘ Ha’penny

Dreadful,”” is one of the directors, were looking for a

chance to get rid of their spirited worlkman who dared

protest against flagrant anti-Catholic bigotry. Now

both men were dismissed under rather similar circum-

stances.  Mr. Partridge was told to withdraw what

he had said and believed to be true; he refused to doso

and was dismissed after many years faithful service;

and Mr. Partridge is a married man with a wife and

young children depending upon him. The dismissal

of Mr. Partridge, if anything, was more tyrannical than

that of Mr. Mansfield, for Mr. Partridge, we under-

stand, only protested to his superiors in office, and did

not even address his shopmates on the matter. Did

Murphy’s ‘‘ Ha'penny Dreadful > come out with scare

headings and eloquent leading articles about the

tyranny of the Great Sourface Railway and the injustice

done to Mr. Partridge? Not at all The owner of

the ‘“ Ha'penny Dreadful,” Mr. William Martin -
Murphy, is a director of the Great Sourface Railway,

and when the Great Sourface Railway acted unjustly

to Mr. Partridge, the *‘Independent’’ did nof see

materials for a sensation in the matter. The teachers

may congratulate themselves that the *“ Independent ™’

feels itself free to join in the chorus of indignation that

the Mansfield dismissal has caused ; and if Murphy has

any spite against Starkie all the better for the teachers,

as there may be more permanence in the campaign, so
far as the ‘‘ Independent '’ is concerned. If Starkie

ran a daily ha’penny paper he is just the man who

could write resounding and rhetorical articles on the

injustice done to Partridge, but we may be sure he
would be as quiet over the Mansfield case as Murphy’s

paper .was over the Partridge dismissal.  Our advice

to the teachers is to keep their heads cool, take

Murphy’s championship in the light of that paper’s
dealing with the Partridge case, and try and restrain

those inexperienced and impetuous teachers who are
easily humbugged by a Joseph Surface, whether he
takes the form of Starkie, the public orator, or a sen-
sational and cheap champion of liberty in the shape of
Murphy’s ‘“ Ha!penny Dreadful.”” The teachers won't
get much help from the ‘‘ Irish Times ' either. Its
report of Iriday night’s meeting did not give the
damaging extracts from Mr. Purser’s letter, and in its
issue on Monday there are only a few inches of space
given to the matter. The teachers manifesto to the
public, which contains the extracts [mm.the ,Purser
letters, was not published in the ‘‘ Irish Times."

The meeting at the Mansion House was a very suc-
cessful and enthusiastic one. ~ We looked in there at
about 9.30, and had to squeeze our way in, so crowded
was the hall. =~ By the newspaper report we see that
Professor Culverwell, of extra-territorial Trinity, pro-
posed some resolutions in the earlier part of the meet-
ing, and. according to one report of his speech he edged
in a reference to his anti-Irish University and got &

_ The extracts from a letter by Mr. Purser, ex-Chief of
- Inspection, were utterly damaging to Starkie, and it
‘seems to us that in face of that letter Starkie should
resign, or, if he refuses, the Commissioners should
resign, or else the letter of the ex-Chief of Inspection
simgxlél be met. Mr. Purser, in the cowrse of his letter,
stated :—

““To my mind the ‘greatest grievance the tea-
chers have is that there should be no means of
appealing against a matter of the sort, and that
the Resident Commissioner or any higher official
should be allowed to intercept and stop an appeal
addressed to the Board. T, as the highest official,
gave my opinion that Mr. had been unfairly
reported on, and was unable to have the report
cancelled or my view of the matter laid before the
Board.””

Here we have' it on the word of the ex-Chief of
Inspection that Starkie is allowed to intercept and
stop an appeal addressed to the Board. Mr. Purser
gave his opinion in a certain case that a teacher was
unfairly reported, and yet his view was not allowed to
reach the Board.

In another paragraph of the letter Mr. Purser em-
phasises the injustice of Starkie. He says:—

“ That the teachers have no means of making
an effective appeal in case of an unjust report
is .perfectly clear and patent after my experience
in the case, but I think that you write im-
prudently when you write of smashing the Board,
and that your Organization is attacking the wrong
party. It is not the Board that is unwilling to
.do justice to the teachers, but Dr. Starkie, who
suppresses facts and reports, and thus prevents
the Board coming to a proper, or any decision.”’

‘The last sentence of this extract from Mr. Purser’s
letter puts Starkie in the dock. Tt expresses the
view that Starkie is unwilling to do justice to the
teachers; in other words, that he is willing to do in-
justice to them, and that Starkie suppresses facts-and
reports and thus prevents the Board from coming to
.a- proper decision. This is a serious indictment
against Starkie—a suppressor of facts and reports, a
man unwilling to do justice to the teachers; and this
person is quite a man of sentiment, a Joseph Surface
in the educational line in public. ~ The Purser letter
_ creates a crisis for the members of the National Board.
They are publicly told by Mr. Purser that Starkie sup-
presses facts and reports, and that he is unwilling to
do justice to the teachers and prevents them—the
Board—from coming to a proper decision. Can the
Commissioners, wishing to be regarded before the
public .as men of honour, leave matters standing as
they are in face of this Jefter?

Having successfully gone through all the prelimi-
navies a certain young Irish Catholic was examined
for a clerkship in the Bank of Ireland: and our readers
need not be told that to get so far is not an easy
madtter for a young man:who happens to be a Catholic.
On June 8rd last Mr. Thos. R. Lamphier, secretary
of the rather exclusive Bank of Ireland—rather exclu-
sive with regard to the staff it lets in but not with
regard to the money it accepts—wrote to the young
man stating that the directors had placed his name on
a list of candidates at the then recent examination for
clerkships to whom they were offering appointments
in the service of the Bank. The young man in due
course was medically examined, and on June 28th Mr.
Thos. R. Lamphier wrote regretting to say that the
medical report-was of such a nature that the Bank was
not able to take him into its service.

As the young man is a stalwart and healthy person
this letter came to him as a great surprise. He
was examined four times by the Bank’s doctor.
On the first occasion there was some suspected imper-
fection, but on subsequent examination the young man
was led to believe that he had passed all right. Almost
immediately he received the letter stating that he was
rejected on the medical report he proceeded to an emi-
nent Merrion Square doctor and had himself examined.
The result of this examination—or rather two exami-
nations, for the young man was examined twice—was
thoroughly satisfactory, and he has a certificate to
that effect. The eminent Merrion Square doctor sug-
gests an explanation in his certificate why the first
examination by the Bank’s doctor did not prove per-
fectly satisfactory, an explanation that by no means
reflected on the young man’s health at the time.

The following day the young man wrote to the Bank
requesting that the medical report be re-considered and
enclosing the certificate of the eminent Merrion Square
doctor. He also pointed out. that he successfully
passed a medical examination four years previously,
.and that during the ensuing period of four years he
-never missed one hour from his business on account
of ill-health : that he had never had to consult a doctor
regarding his health, and that he had never been medi-
_cally treated since he was six years old.

* That letter was sent on-July 8rd, and on July 11th
the secretary of the Bank. of Ireland wrote- that the
letter had been submitted to the governors and direc-
tors and that they could not see their way to alter
their decision refusing to take this young man into their
service. The young man wrote again stating his case.
He pointed out that though the Bank’s doctor was not
thoroughly satisfied at the first examination he was
evidently satisfied with his condition on the other three
occasions. He also pointed out that on being informed
that the first report was unsatisfactory he con-
sulted a doctor—not the Merrion Square one this time
—and this doctor could not trace any -ailment in him.
On August 2nd the secretary replied in a brief stereo
typed sort. of letter regretting that the directors did
not consider there was any reason why they should
alter their-previous decision.  Yet again the young
man wrote, and on August 14th the assistant secretary
of the Bank of Ireland replied curtly that there was
nothing to add to the previous letter. On August 17
the young man wrote for the return of his birth certi-
ficate, the certificate of the Merrion Square doctor and
for his references. And on the 19th August the docu-
ments received by the Bank were returned and. there
was an end to the matter.

But should the matter be allowed to end here ? Here
is a young Catholic Irishman, with more than ample
evidence that he was in sound health, rejected by the
Bank of Ireland on a medical report. His future may
‘be compromised by the fact that a bank rejected him
on the ground of health. His health as a medical certi-
ficate shows was all right; as he is a Catholic perhaps
the Bank did not consider that his soul was quite
sound enough for this hotbed of Ascendancy.

We did not waste our time reading all. the stuff
spouted by Messrs. Moore, Craig and Co. in the Bri-

amendment. The amendment was badly beaten. But
we see that the eloquent Mr. Birrell expressed his-re-
gret at ‘‘ the modified form of compulsion that was
‘employed. by the new University '’ : he added that they
Englishmen had ‘‘ no right of their own notion and of
themselves to expect other nations and
across the water, with other ideas and ideals, to let
them thrust their own: opinions down the throats of
those peoples.”” * The latter quotation is as good as the
sentimental Starkie might perpetrate. But why when
it comes to the verb to do is the eloquent Birrell trying
to interfere with the Essential Irish position by seeking
to force a breach of it by the weapon of a bribe to the
Gounty Councils? County Councils that have decided
for Essential Trish in connection with their scholarships
are, if the nimble Birrell is to have his way—but he
won’t—to be forced to make certain of their scholar-
ships tenable at any University. The Home Rule
Samuel succeeded in dumping Norway over lere: bub
we do not believé that the nimble Birrell, even though

other peoples

ing down or humbugging our' Nationalist County Coun-
cils that stand for Essential Trish.

we would give it we could say, as the mistresses some-
times say in the ‘‘papers’’ they give their hgusemqgdsy,l

There has been some silly logic-chopping going on
about Trinity being allowed to leave her situation. It
is asked if Trinity, why not Belfast. That is a dlﬁe-

is that any reason why they should get four counties

The ‘‘ Freeman '’ gave itself away very stupidly in
one sentence in its leading article of October 24th.
Discussing the ** Irish Times ' in. connection with the
Trinity blunder it says:—"‘ As journalists we heartily
sympathise with our contemporary in the pam'ful
wriggling which the maintenance of this paradox im-
poses on it.”’”  What a burst of unconscious frankness
for the poor * Freeman.”” It has done, and no floubt
will do, so many wriggles itself that our magnanimous
contemporary actually sympathises with tricky old Alf
Fox!

The ‘‘ Freeman,’’ by the way, says that it never
approved of County Councils making their scholarships
tenable only at constituent Colleges of the National
University.  The public had better note that admis-
sion. In this view the ‘ Freeman’ is greatly out of
touch with sound Nationalist opinion. Owing to a
very loose construction we are in doubt if we are right
in stating that the ‘‘ Freeman’’ also asserts that it
never approved County Councils making Irish: compul-
sory for their scholarships. It seems to us that it
commits itself to that view also.
quote the part from the ‘Freeman,
readers decide for themselves:—

Some of the County Councils have made Irish

We have never approved of this action of the

The ‘“Freeman ’ apparently is a child in these

set at rest by the Executive Committe~ of the Irish Co.

In the first place, the acceptance of the con-

This is clearly an agreement which should not
be lightly disregarded or set aside, and it is equally

bargain and to resist all inducements to violate it.

In our issue of October 19th we published an article
from a Spaniard, the descendant of an old Irish family.
The suggestion it contained that a certain class of
young men should be waiters was worth, we considered,
placing before the Irish public whose ‘‘ pride of race "
is so well and comically known to us all.  We have
no wish to induce persons to leave Ireland for Spain,
and it was not with the object of inducing anything of
the sort that we published the article. — This week
one who has been in Spain draws another and less
glowing picture of the prospects there for some people.
We have received letters from various parts of Treland,
from Wexford, Clare, Galway, Cork, Cavan, from
young men anxious to go to Spain—how smart they
are to jump at an offer to leave the country! If young
men would do what is to be done, as they have to do
when they go' to foreign countries, and use their heads,
there are plenty of openings for them in Ireland; and
Ireland is bound to rapidly develop under Home Rule.
If young men want to leave Ireland and go to Spain we
cannot stop them, but we see no reason why we should
help them. We cannot see our way-to place our corre-
spondents into communication with ““ Ultonia,’” as we
did not publish the latter’s letter with any view to
constituting ourselves an agency for promoting emigra-
tion to Spain or anywhere else.

We have been requested by Messrs. Hicks, Bullick
and Co., Ltd., Sackville Thread Works, Bélfast, to
publish the following letter:—‘ Catholic Workers in
Belfast. Sir,—It came to our knowledge yesterday
that our firm’s name is on a list circulated amongst
drapers, etc., over the Midlands, South and West of
Ireland, from which it is inferred that we have dis-
missed our Roman Catholic workers. We wish to
state that this is an enfire falsehood, nothing of the
sort has been either done or thought of, all our em-
ployees are treated alike with fairness and impartiality
whatever their religion. We have no sympathy with
any other course of action, and we are quite at a loss
to know of any justification for the spreading of above
untruth regarding our company, or any othér local
ss place, while as to intimidation by fellow-
i workers (which is possibly the origin of the story), we
]

have not heard of any cases of the like except in the
shipyards.  You will understand that as we arve the
only makers of sewing cottons in Ireland, such a report
going about would tend to damage, and without reason,
an Irish industry.—We are, yours faithfully (for Messrs.
| Hicks, Bullick and Co., Ltd), N. BuLuick, Director.
| October 22, 1912.”

We publish this letter at the request of Messrs.

as there has been no reference to them in these columns

we don’t see that we are strictly called upon to inter-

dismissed their Catholic workers.

i This letter and the notovious Belfast atrocities and
the refusal of the manufacturers of the North-East and
of the so-called ** scattered Protestants’ of the *“South
and West”’ to protest against and denounce the sava-
gery meted out to many unfortunate work people be-
cause they happen to be Catholics, or as they are
called, ‘‘ Fenians,” suggest many reflections, and we
gladly seize upon the opportunity given us by the letter

of Messrs. Hicks, Bullick and Co., Ltd., of Belfast, to

put down some of them.

Messrs. Hicks, Bullick and Co. state they ™ have no
sympathy”’ with a course of action that leads to the
dismissal of workers because they are Catholics: and
they state that in view of a state of affairs which they
think tends to damage their business in its capacity as
““an TIrish industry.”

First of all let us imagine—and you can imagine
4 = many things -that are practically impossible—let us
2l imagine that the non-Catholic and Unionist employees
of some firms in Dublin were treated as were many
(atholic and some non-Catholic Home Rule workers in
Belfast. Imagine that hundreds or thousands of Pro-
testants in Dublin dare not appear at work: that an
attempt was made to roast one of them: that many
were beaten and chased in the streets, and that hun-
dreds and thousands of homes were left with their
breadwinners idle and penniless because of a savagery
and -barbarity in Dublin that would not :_\llow [OmME
 unfortunate Protestant workers to earn their bread by

 the sweat of their brows. Tmagine {lu_ﬂ‘ impossible
state of things. What would be the inevitable g sulf ?

Within forty-eight hours a grent meeting would be
called by Catholics to denounce the atrocitics, the
Jatholic archbishop and clergy would denounce them,
ﬂ‘:h‘e Catholic business fitms would denounce them, and
blin 1d ring with denunciations of a state of
v which would be acclaimed by all respectable and

responsible Catholics as bringing disgrace upon the

Now there is no need to ask people to imagine such
a state of atrocities existing in Belfast. I'or it has
existed, and though things have cooled down—and per-
haps the cooling is not altogether disconnected from
action in the ‘‘South and West '—there has been no
meeting of Belfast Protestants called to denounce the
conduct that has made the name of Protestant Belfass
stink in the nostrils of the liberal-minded world.

Surely Belfast cannot be surprised that Ireland re-
sents its atrocities if it fails promptly and adequabely
to resent them themselves. ~From Unionist Belfast,
and places akin to it in feeling, a stream of slander is
kept up about ‘‘ the South and West,”” and with all

land. In addition there is a torrent of wordy con-

anti-Home Rule interest. It is called, ‘‘ Ulster's

a_ ha r holding Treland. Ulster is' styled ** Pros-

it must be a matter of little or no concern to Belfast
in its ** Prosperity Province '’ if ‘* Poverty Province "

facturers cannot have it both ways. Unionist Belfast
is threatening civil war—of course it; is playing the fool,

Ireland gets Home Rule, but when it is feared that
Ireland may hit back then Ireland is reminded by our
correspondents, Messrs. Hicks, Bullick and Co., Ltd.,
that to hit them is to hit ‘‘ an Irish industry.”” We
know nothing of the views concerning Ireland held by
this firm : its members may be Nationalists for all we
know, but we presume they are Unionists. If anyone
has said that they dismissed their Catholic workers
in face of the fact that they have not done so, it is a
gross injustice to them. They have not sent us the
circular to which they refer. But if Belfast is being
hit at how can Belfast be surprised ?

Nothing is bad enough to say against the ‘ South
and West,”” and we see one of their postcards insult-
ingly paints up Connacht as ‘‘ Poverty Province.”” Re-
fugees have run in terror from Belfast, Catholics have
been assaulted and forcibly kept from earning their
bread when employers were willing to employ them—
we don’t refer now to the great discrimination against
Catholics seeking for employment which is carried out
in Belfast without violence and on the quiet. Messrs.
Hicks, Bullick state that they have no sympathy
with unfairmess to Catholic workers. It seems
to us that the situation of which the Belfast atrocities
are such a prominent feature called for, and still calls
for, a vigorous and real denunciation on the part of all
respectable Protestants in Belfast and all over Ireland,
and more particularly from Belfast manufacturers,
who are doing a large trade all over Ireland, a trade
that has been, we have no doubt, considerably ex-
panded by the Trish industrial movement started by the
TEADER in the much abused ‘‘South and West.”” " The -
“ South and West”” has had to subscribe for the up-
keep of the vietims of Belfast blackguardism, and we

ment against Belfast of the atrocities. Surely Belfast

firms can understand it also; for they cannot be so

and have no natural resentments left in them.

HALLOW E’EN REVELS.

Vl‘ HIS grand old Irish festival has come round once
more. Sambhain is here again. The sea-divided
Gael welcomes Hallow K’en with almost the same
enthusiasm as he does St. Patrick’s Day.  Where
would one expect to find it more religiously kept than
“among the Irish Members temporarily exiled in Lon-
don?  We use the word Irish in its fullest sense,
stretehing it to include not merely the South and West
but even ‘‘ Ulster.”” Nay, we throw in Trinity Col-
lege, although recent developments in the British
House of Commons have isolated that area in the
centre of Dublin from the country more completely
than the counties of the ‘‘ Covenant.”” An uninformed
but inquiring mind might ask, ‘“ What of the Bast?"”
He is unaware that recent geographical researches have
disclosed the fact that Ireland is unique among the
nations in being composed of only North, South and
West,  Presumably the East disappeared in West
Britain during the past century. ~
Be that as it may, it affords us pleasure to print some
information as to the pledsant programme, or Clar,
for the Ceilidh to be held on Hallow E’en by our Mem-
bers across the Irish Sea. Pride of place is accorded
to the ancient pastime of ‘‘ burning nuts.” All of us
are, no doubt, familiar with this amusing game. A
pair of nuts (we refer of course to the indigestible
comestible of men and monkeys) is placed on the hob,
or top bar of the fireplace. The nuts are named after
certain persons the state of whose affections towards
one another has become a matler of gossip or jest in
the merry company. An omen is sought. Should the
nuts burn down together the fidelity of the pair is
assured. If they.leap apart, or if one jumps from the
other, inconstancy is predicted. This is such a simple
game that all can play it. Siv Bdward Carson, we
understand from a Nationalist M.P., intends to put
down two nuts, named, respectively, ‘‘ Ulster ”’ and
‘‘ Ireland.” He is confident that * Ulster '’ will im-
mediately jump away from her companion, and he
has, we believe, threatened that if she seems slow
about doing so he will push her off with his blackthorn.
This seems hardly playing the game, but of course it
may be all bluff. We shall see. M. J. H. Campbell
has a pretty idea, as we learn from our informant.
The nuts will be called “ Trinity > and ‘ Ireland.”
(These patriots simply cannot forego playing with the
good name of their country.) The distinguished law-
yor predicts gleefully that ‘“ Trinity ’ will fall into
the fire, though what it is to gain by so doing it is
difiicult to see. Capt. Craig, our friend tells us, is
making play with ‘‘ Ireland ”’ and ‘‘ Empire.”” He
expects ‘“ Ireland ’ to quit her partner at once, and
will be much disappointed if she does not do so. Alto-
gether great fun may be looked for during the progress
of this pastime.

The Nationalists are striking out in another line, if
we may believe a Unionist member. Diving head-
down into a pail or bucket of water will be their amuse-
ment. They do not do this to cool their heads, as
might be imagined. They have a befter reason. The
pail will be filled with good things, and the desire of
the divers is to pick up as much of the spoil as they
can. The competition is pretty sure to be keen. The
TUnionists are disposed to complain that they will not
be given a fair “do’’ at this game. They imply that

the Nationalists intend to muster in force round the ~
pail and do all the spoil-snatching themselves. This
the Nationalists deny, but some: of them have been
heard to say that they were not given a fair chance ag
this particular game on other occasions {md that it is
““up to them * to make theimost 40[ b}xenr present op-
portunity. It is a pity that anything in the nature of
unfriendly rivalry should mar these festivities, s0 ad-
mirably designed to bring together all creeds and
classes.

The famous sport of snap-apple, however, seems
splendidly suited to give everyone a chance. Dodging
the light and capturing the fruit is, no doubt, rather a
job, but that very fact naturally enhances the interest,
especially when' the players are numerous. If the
game is played according to the (EHlome) Rules the
sport should be considerable. Statements have beern
made that the fruit will be nothing like as fine as in
previous years, lacking the juicy qualities, and decid-
edly smaller in size. Green apples, of course, cannot
compare with red, and the noted partiality of the Nor-
therns for the Orange must also be borne in mind.

This reminds us that in addition to the games we
have alluded to, there will be a feast of treason and a
flow of dole. ‘‘ Gipsy nuts’’ with their amusing mottoes
will grace the board in profusion:  We understand
that some of the mottoes are very amusing, and should
provoke much merriment if they fall into wrong hands.
Thus ‘“ Ulster will fight, Ulster will be right,”” * Hands
off Trinity,”” “ No man has a right to set bounds to the
march of a nation,”” *‘ Quis separabit,”” to give a few
samples, may easily set the whole company agog.
Apples, nuts (they are addicted to nuts in Belfast),
grapes (sour and otherwise), and every class of rare
and refreshing fruit from Lloyd George’s hot (Lime}y
house will also be provided. The doctors are sure to
be busy later.

Nor should we forget to mention the barmbrack
without which no feast on Hallow I’en may be deemed
complete. The colcannon pertains more to dinner,
but the barmbrack with its hidden ring is the feature
of the evening feast. Arrangements have been made
with Mr. Asquith to cut the cake, and the scramble
for the lucky piece containing the golden circlet will
then begin. At present Unionist members profess an
indifference to the chance of securing the much:
coveted morsel with its precious prize. \Whether they
will succeed in maintaining that aloof attitude once
the cake has been carved remains to be seen. The
Unionists at present are opposed to the cutting of the
cake at all. What they call ‘‘ the dismemberment
is in their opinion unnecessary. It is easy to find

THE IDEAL OF A CATHOLIC TRUTH SOCIETY.

HE trouble with us Catholics is that we so often
fail to realise the possibilities of our institutions.
The Trish Catholic Truth Society, for instance, might
become a dominant factor in the life of the nation. It
might bring a whole phalanx of cultured ¢lerics and
well-read laymen into the field, to do battle on behalf
of Faith and Fatherland.
The Catholic Truth Society might become, and
should become, a corporate Maecenas for the encour-
. agement and direction of Irish Catholic talent, .which
_ at._present lies idle and unproductive. It might stir
up scores of talented young priests, and scores of edu-
cated laymen, to do good service with the pen on be-
half of Catholic truth. It might help, more potently
than any other agency, in putting an end to the glacial
period of stagnation in matters of scholarship, among
the Trish clergy; it might do much to remove the re-
proach of literary barrenness from Catholic Ireland.
B might save thousands of Trish Catholic wage-

carners from being led astray by the delusive dreams
of infidel Socialism. It might do admirable work in
promoting the many social reforms—housing reform
and temperance reform, to mention bub two examples
—which are so urgently needed in Catholic Ireland.

It might foster the production of a varied and in-
structive Catholic literature, brightly and entertain-
ingly written, which would serve as a healthy substi-
tufe for the morbid and deleterious output of the de-
cadent English Press. Finally, and above all, the
Irish Catholic Truth Society might become a tower of
strength to the millions of our Catholic brethren who
are scatbered among Protestant majorities through-
out the English-speaking world. It might furnish the
Catholics of Canada and the United States, of South.
Africa and Australia, with effective antidotes to the
myriads of Protestant and Rationalist misrepresenta-
tions which annually confront them.

Reader, have you ever lived for any considerable
time in one of the bustling and progressive over-sea
dominions of the British Empire—in Australia for ex.-

ample? If 5o, you can hardly have failed to observe
the fact that, week by week and year by year, a verit-
-able torrent of non-Catholic literature—much of it
flagrantly anti-Catholic—pours from England and
-Scotland into every part-of the English-speaking world.
And the heart-breaking feature of the business is, that
dor all this mighty torrent of Protestant and infidel
- literature, Castholic Ireland provides no antidote what-
:soever.

The public has a vight to expect, our Catholic bre-

_thren abroad have a right to expect, that the Ivish
‘Catholic Truth Society should do something —and it
«can do a great deal—to remedy this lamentable state
of affairs. The public has also a right to look for a
-considerable improvement in the working of the Society
during the coming year. The Society has recently
‘been the recipient of a remarkable series of cold douches
-of criticism ; and these strictures have come, not from
foes, but from friends and well-wishers. Cold douches
are said to produce a tonic and bracing effect upon the
nervous system. Let us hope that the recent douches
-of criticism, pouring in from so many frienaly quarters,
will have a bracing effect upon the Society, and will
rouse it to more energetic action in the coming year.

The annual Catholic Truth Conference should not
‘be a languid formality, flavoured by mutual admira-
‘tion. It should be a regular Catholic Congress, as it
already is among the Catholics of England.  Such a
-Congress would arouse far more enthusiasm, and pro-
duce vastly better results, than the yearly Conference
<does at present. A Catholic Congress, properly or-
ganised, would prove an inestimable boon to the Irish
‘Church. Tt would enlist the services of many sterling
<Catholics whose energies are now frittered away upon
other things. It would call forth many an important
-contribution to Catholic literature. It would open out
mnew vistas of improvement; it would lead to a prac-
tical striving after better things, especially in the social
order. Finally, it would bring about a greater esprit
de corps among Irish Catholics; it would knit them
more firmly m the bonds of brotherhood ; it would fill

* _them with a legitimate pride in the Faith they profess.
‘We can well afford to have a Katolikentag in Ireland
=very year, even as our brother-Catholics have in Ger-

many. The Catholics of Australia manage to hold a

very successful Catholic Congress once in three years,

despite the enormous distances which the delegates

‘haye to travel. Besides, in every State of the Austra-

lian Commonwealth, congresses are held yearly (under

-various names) by the Anglicans, the Methodists, the

Baptists, the Congregationalists, and so on. At these

«congresses, papers are. read on a variety of subjects;

‘speeches and addresses are delivered; and public

«opinion is formed, as far as possible, in one direction

or another. Why not have a similar outlet for Catholic

:zeal in this country ?  With a little energy on the part

of the Irish Catholic Truth Society, aided by a few

~capable organisers, we could have a splendid Catholic
- “Congress each year in-Ireland. Surely it 1s unreason-
~ -able to suppose that we Irish Catholics are so stagnant,

50 heedless of our interests as Catholics, that we can-

not manage to hold an important Congress once a year

‘in Dublin or elsewhere.

The Catholic Truth Society can do a great work by
~ -organising a successful Catholic Congress once a year.

It can do a still greater work by securing as many

-able contributors as possible, and publishing a multi-

tude of fresh and interesting pamphlets in the course

of the year. At present, one hears numerous com-

horse, she again set out, transferred her patients to the

hospital, which she reached by evening, left her patients
in the hands of the nuns, rested briefly in the church porch,
the people fearing to admit her, and got back as far as
Blackwater, where she collapsed and was found and
sheltered by the local coastguards after forty-eight hours’
strenuous toil and the responsibility of a horse and van
so laden day and night

conbinues to decline. From Munster’s comparatively
' “slight increase in.non-productives (mostly women and

- To sum up, the population of Treland which was
4,458,775 persons ‘in 1901 had declined to 4,390,219
~on 2nd April, 1911, a falling off of only 68,556 persons,
or 1.5 per cent., a slightly lower decline than the first
reburns showed. It was generally inferred from the
figures first published, and pending the publication of
-details, that the turning point had been almost reached
and ‘that the result was fairly satisfacto This ex-
pectation has not been realised. The agricultural, the
domestic, and the industrial classes have all heavily
declined, not merely in certain sterile and backward
portions of the country, but all over it. As for the
agricultural exodus nothing seems ‘to check it, or so
much as to influence it—and this after all the heroic
efforts of the Imperial Parliament for forty years and
more! There are to be sure a few details of minor
concern revealed in the summaries of a satisfactory

nature. Pauperism has declined in all four provinces.

So also has. illiteracy. Education has increased, and

has perhaps tended to swell the number of aspirants
for the learned professions, at the expense of trade and

agriculture. -

In connection with education in Ireland the follow-
ing frightful case of hardship is instanced by the Right
Hon. T. Sinclair in an article included in the pam-
phlet ‘‘ Against Home Rule.”” Tt is given as a sample
of what Protestants may expect in Ireland once Home
Rule is granted.

““ A few years ago a Protestant member of a public
service was transferred upon promotion from Belfast
to a Roman Catholic district in which his boys had no
available school but that of the Christian Brothers,
and his girls none but that of the local convent.’”’  But
would it not have been better to have appointed a
Catholic to the vacancy and reserved the Belfast Pro-
testant for a different sort of locality ? The present
writer was under the. impression that the National
Board have schools in every district all over Ireland,
but it would seem this is not so if we are to believe the
Right Hon. T. Sinclair.

The Unionist Associations of Ireland in their recently
published ‘‘ Nutshell ”’ in reply to that of Mr. J. Mac-
Veagh, M.P., are at great pains to press forward the
argument from prosperity.

Mr. T. P. Gill, Secretary of the Department of Agri-
culture, is quoted from as follows:—‘ The time has
come for us to take account of the fact, which for the
past ten years has been steadily emerging into view,
and to proclaim this fact to ourselves and to the world
that Treland to-day is not only a progressive nation,

 THE LEADER.

but, within her own limits, amongst the most rapidly
and soundly progressive nations in the world.” s

This is on page 102 and is taken from a speech deli-
vered last year at Belfast. At the top of the same
page we get the following :—

““If we take the ten years ending the 31st March,
1910, we find that in Treland there was an increase in
the gross assessments of income derived from business
concerns, professions, and employments of 80.4. per
cent., the corresponding increase for Ingland being
20.2 per cent., and for Scotland only 18.9 per cent.
It is clear from these figures that Ireland has pro-
gressed at a much more rapid rate than either England
or Scotland.”’

Just so--but why then have the persons whé en-
joyed this phenomenal prosperity reduced the number
of their domestic servants

In support of Mr. T. P. Gill the increase in the trade
at Irish ports is instanced, viz., £6,162,837, of 1910

over 1909. This increase is thus made up :—
Exports ... £4.,898,069
Imports e 1,264,768

The result is that Treland now exports more than she
imports! Wholly unconscious of having arrived at a
reductio ad absurdum, the ‘‘ Nutshell ’ proceeds thus:

““The figures for 1910 show that the external trade
of Treland, per head of the population, represents:—
Imports, £14 6s. 9d., and exports, £14 10s. 5d. The
corresponding figures for the United Kingdom are £12
12s. 8d. and £9 9s. 4d.””

Assuming the two islands to be both prospering, it
is clear that the respective prosperities are of exactly
opposite descriptions. English and Scotch prosperity
takes the form of parting with £9 and getting back
€12, the ordinary humdrum method characteristic of
trading nations, the Irish method (exporting £14 10s.
ad. worth, and getting back £14 6s. 9d.) being experi-
mental, and worth watching!

By way of emphasising the prosperity argument the
““ Nutshell " concludes as follows i—

‘“There are only three Furopean countries, namely,
Belgium, Holland, and Switzerland, which show a
larger volume of trade per head of the population than
Treland.”’

Belgium and Holland, though independent States,
are geographically part and parcel of Germany. A
large proportion of their trade is one of pure transit for
German goods. Its increasing volume, accompanied
by a steady influx of Germans into Antwerp, Rotter-
dam and other centres along the waterways which con-
nect them with their mighty neighbour, has been for
some years a source of grave anxiety to Belgian and
Dutch statesmen.

The illustration is pointless, Ireland having no
transit trade belonging to any other country, nor
indeed very much of her own!

: BENCHERS AND BIGOTRY.

THE Benchers of the King’s Inns are hvmg up to

their motto. It is thirty-six years since they
earned unenviable notoriety by refusing to admit to
the Irish Bar the late A. M. Sullivan, M.P. The reason
of the refusal was that Mr. Sullivan was an Irish Nation-
alist who had devoted his talents to the service of his
countrymen and had refused to touch his hat or bend
his knee to the anti-Irish ascendancy. Their excuse
was that Mr. Sullivan was technically in default with
regard to the number of lectures he should have attendedy
and that notwithstanding the recommendation of his
lecturers and examiners it would he a matter of grace
to admit him under the circumstances, and they did not
choose to ‘be gracious. To sprigs of Tory * gentry”
the grace was usually accorded, even when lectures
were missed for reasons less worthy than attention to
Parliamentary duty, but the minds of the Benchers
were most naturally shocked at the idea of treating a
vulgar Irish Nationalist with the same consideration as
the son of a squireen, and Mr. Sullivan’s memorial was
rejected. The result was curious. This irresponsible
body was unmoved by the outery of public indignation,
but the English Inns,of Court resolved to admir
a slap in the face to the *‘ Irish ”’ bigots. A special
complimentary call was at once accorded to Mr. Sullivan
in the Inner Temple, and he was without examination
admitted to the I W Bar, as a protest against the
insolence of the body that refused him admission to the
profession in his own country. Mr. Sullivan emigrated
t0 England, and though subsequently * called” in
Dublin he never returned save for the defence of Pat
Egan in the State Trials of 1882. Ilis speech on that
occasion is still 16 nembered as an event of yesterday.
His son Mr. A. M. Sullivan. was last July appointed
to the position of Third Serjeant-at-Law in Ireland.
As a matter of ccurse it was umed that Mr. Sullivan
would be co-opted at the next meeting of the Benchers.
There is no known instance in the history of the office
in which nomination to the Serjeancy has not been fol-
lowed at once by co-option of the Benchers, where the
< new Serjeant was not -already a member of that body.

; Never before, however, has the executive ventured to

offend the susceptibilities of these gentlemen by appointing
an Irish Nationalist to the office. The audacity of the
Crown has now been fittingly rebuked. The vacancy in
the august roll of Benchers of the King’s Inns has been
filled by co-option—not of His Majesty’s Third Serjeant,
but of Andy Horner. This gentleman’s qualification
consists in the immoderation with which he abuses
& Irishmen, and the extravagance of his incitements to
8 } armed resistance t¢c Home Rule. Had this treatment
been accorded to a Nationalist nobody public comment
would have been unrestrained. Serjeant Sullivan, how-
ever, is connected by marriage of his first cousin with
i ‘T. M. Healy, M.P., and before the step was taken of
refusing to co-opt him one of the most prominent coer-
cionist Benchers had been assured by a * Nationalist
Crown Prosecutor that to such degradation had Irish
journalism come that no comment would be made in
the_Nationalist Press on the insolent scandal of the pro-
ceedings. This estimate of the daily Press was justified
by the mean and discreditable comment published in
- * the Freeman’s Journal on Saturday. But even this was
too much for the disappointment and jealousy of the
gentleman who anonymously communicated to the
TFreeman on Monday morning an attack on A. M. Sullivan
and a defence of the Benchers of the King’s Inns. It

% takes a long time to emancipate an Irish slave.
